title: ⭐⭐ Revision (exam type): the trip so far

questions.prompt: Complete: "Anis ___ English with the Bakers every day."
questions.explanation: The story is in the past, and "speak" is irregular: its simple past is "spoke". "Speaked" and "spoked" wrongly add -ed to an irregular verb, and "speak" is the base form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Yesterday at noon, Anis and Oliver ___ lunch in a park."
questions.explanation: An action in progress at a past moment (noon yesterday) takes the past continuous. The subject is plural, so "were eating". "Was eating" is singular, "were eat" forgets the -ing, and "are eating" is present.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Big Ben is a very ___ monument; millions of tourists know it."
questions.explanation: Something everybody knows is "famous". "Crowded" means full of people, "noisy" means full of sound, and "modern" means new — but Big Ben is famous and old, not new.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Oliver never gets angry and always smiles. He is very ___."
questions.explanation: A person who always smiles and is happy is "cheerful". "Shy" means afraid to talk, "rude" means not polite, and "short" describes the body, not the character.

questions.prompt: Complete: "July in Bizerte is ___ than in London."
questions.explanation: "Hot" is a short adjective ending in consonant-vowel-consonant, so we double the t and add -er: "hotter than" ✓. "Hoter" forgets the double t, "more hot" is wrong for a short adjective, and "hottest" is for three or more.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The streets of London are ___ than the streets of Bizerte."
questions.explanation: "Busy" ends in -y, so the comparative changes -y to -ier: "busier than" ✓. "Busyer" keeps the y wrongly, "more busy" is wrong for a short -y adjective, and "busiest" is for three or more.
